goal: Review the proof and issues for rm-00332; agree on the key remaining gaps and an action plan.

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
I have to be honest about something up front: this problem is not in my wheelhouse. This is a problem about supercongruences for the Domb/Ramanujan-type series $\sum \binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$, Bernoulli and Euler numbers, and $p$-adic valuations. There's no Laplacian here, no spectral gap, no effective resistance to compute. My instinct is always to ask "can we find this by solving $Lx=b$?" — but here the right tools are $p$-adic analysis, the Wilf–Zeilberger method, and Gauss/Jacobi sums, not spectral graph theory.

Let me nonetheless say what I *can* contribute structurally, because some of this is algorithmic:

1. **Part (v) is the one I'd actually attack constructively.** The integrality of $\frac{1}{2n\binom{2n}{n}}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3 16^{n-1-k}$ is a statement that a certain explicit integer sequence is divisible by $2n\binom{2n}{n}$. That's exactly the kind of thing the **WZ (Wilf–Zeilberger) method** settles algorithmically: find a closed-form certificate / recurrence for $S(n)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3 16^{n-1-k}$, then verify $2n\binom{2n}{n}\mid S(n)$ by tracking $p$-adic valuations prime-by-prime via Kummer's theorem. I'd want to see the recurrence written down explicitly before believing the existence proof.

2. **Parts (i)–(iv) should be reduced to a known engine.** Parts (i),(ii) are clearly the van Hamme / Mortenson style results connecting $\sum \binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k \bmod p^2$ to the representation $p=x^2+3y^2$ — these come from formulas for Gaussian hypergeometric ${}_3F_2$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$ and Jacobi sums. The action item is to pin down the exact reference and check the $p^2$ refinement, not just the $p$ congruence.

3. **The genuinely delicate parts are (iii) and (iv)** because of the $B_{p-3}$ and $E_{p-3}$ terms at level $p^3$/$p^4$. Those require a careful $p$-adic expansion — the supercongruence engine of Z.-W. Sun's program. The "gap" most likely lives here: getting the constant $7/6$ and the $E_{p-3}$ coefficient $2(\frac{-1}{p})$ exactly right modulo $p^4$.

So my honest action plan: someone with the WZ/Sun toolkit should drive. The one place my taste is useful is demanding an **explicit certificate** for (v) rather than an existence argument — show me the recurrence and the valuation bookkeeping. What does the current draft proof actually use for (iii)?

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan is right that this is not a spectral problem — and I will say candidly it is not a graph-limits problem either. My theta-function and graphon machinery have no purchase on supercongruences for $\sum \binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$. Let me be disciplined about where my actual tools *do* help, rather than forcing an analogy.

A few precise points:

1. **On (v), I agree with Dan that WZ is the engine, but I want to sharpen the bookkeeping.** Write $T(n)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3 16^{n-1-k}$. The divisibility $2n\binom{2n}{n}\mid T(n)$ is *not* a single prime statement — it is a uniform statement across all primes, so the clean way is Kummer/Legendre: for each prime $q$, $v_q\!\big(2n\binom{2n}{n}\big) = v_q(2n) + (\text{number of carries adding } n+n \text{ in base } q)$. One must show $v_q(T(n)) \ge$ that for every $q$ simultaneously. The WZ recurrence converts this into a finite induction, but I would insist the recurrence be **first-order or second-order with polynomial coefficients in $n$**, so that the valuation tracking is genuinely finite. Without that the "existence" claim is empty.

2. **On (i)–(ii): the $p=x^2+3y^2$ structure is the right extremal object here.** This is the supersingular/CM picture — $p\equiv 1\pmod 6$ splits in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$, and $4x^2-2p$ is essentially $2\operatorname{Re}(\pi)^2$-type data for the Hecke character. The $p^2$ refinement is exactly Mortenson's and Z.-W. Sun's strength. The check I want: that the *second* congruence $\sum k\binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k \equiv (p-4x^2)/3$ is genuinely independent or whether it follows from the first by a derivative/contiguity relation on the ${}_3F_2$. If it follows formally, (ii)'s companion $\equiv p/3$ should fall out of the same relation by setting $x$ to its $p\equiv 5$ behavior — that would economize the proof considerably.

3. **(iii) and (iv) are where the real gap lives, as Dan said.** The constants $7/6$ and $2(\frac{-1}{p})$ at level $p^3$, $p^4$ come from the $p$-adic Gamma / Euler-number expansion, and these are the kind of coefficients that are wrong in the literature one time in five. I'd demand the explicit intermediate identity expressing $\sum_{k=0}^{p^a-1}(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$ via a $p$-adic analytic function whose Taylor coefficients are $B_{p-3}$ and $E_{p-3}$, before trusting the constants.

So my proposed action plan: (a) get the explicit WZ recurrence for (v) on the board; (b) determine whether (i) $\Rightarrow$ its companion and (ii) by a contiguity relation; (c) produce the $p$-adic expansion underlying (iii)–(iv) and verify $7/6$ and the Euler coefficient by hand for $p=5,7,11$. What does the current draft actually invoke for (iii) — a Sun-style lemma, or is the constant asserted without derivation?

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Good — Dan and László have framed this well, and I won't pretend my trace formula or QUE machinery touches these supercongruences directly. But let me push on the *real* theorem behind the technical one, because there's a beautiful structure here that the WZ bookkeeping obscures.

The series $\sum \binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$ is the truncated period of a Hecke eigenform — this is the CM elliptic curve / modular form attached to $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$, and that is *exactly* why $p=x^2+3y^2$ appears. So before anyone WZ-certifies, let me state what I think is actually going on:

1. **(i)/(ii) are a Ramanujan phenomenon in disguise.** The quantity $4x^2-2p$ is $a_p^2 - 2p$ where $a_p$ is the Hecke eigenvalue of the weight-2 CM form of conductor 36 (or the relevant level). Indeed for $p\equiv 1 \pmod 6$ split in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$, write $\pi=x+y\sqrt{-3}$, $a_p = 2x = \pi+\bar\pi$, so $a_p^2 = 4x^2$, and $4x^2-2p = a_p^2-2p = \alpha_p^2+\beta_p^2$ with $\alpha_p\beta_p=p$. For $p\equiv 5\pmod 6$ the form is *supersingular*, $a_p=0$, and you get $\equiv 0 \bmod p^2$ instantly — that's exactly case (ii)! So I'd bet the companion sum $\sum k\binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$ is the *derivative* of the period in the weight/twist parameter — László's contiguity instinct is correct, and the $\frac{p}{3}$ in (ii) is just the supersingular limit of $(p-4x^2)/3$ with $4x^2\to 0$. Let me check $p=7$: $7=2^2+3\cdot1^2$, $x=2$, so $4x^2-2p=16-14=2$. One should verify $\sum_{k=0}^{6}\binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k \equiv 2 \pmod{49}$ by hand — that's the kind of explicit anchor I always insist on.

2. **The $B_{p-3}$ in (iii) and $E_{p-3}$ in (iv) are the same beast at the next order.** $B_{p-3}$ is the $p$-adic $L$-value $L_p(-2,\omega^{\text{even}})$, essentially $\zeta_p(3)$ data; $E_{p-3}$ is the analogous $p$-adic value attached to the *quadratic* twist $(\frac{-1}{p})$ — note the Euler number signals the imaginary quadratic field $\mathbb{Q}(i)$, while (iii) with Bernoulli is the trivial/even part. That dichotomy (Bernoulli for the full sum to $p^a$, Euler for the half-sum to $(p-1)/2$) is precisely a real-vs-imaginary place distinction. So the constant $\frac{7}{6}$ should be *derivable*, not asserted: it's a combination of $\frac{1}{6}$-type Hurwitz-zeta constants from the $p$-adic Gamma expansion of $\binom{2k}{k}$. I fully endorse László's demand: compute it for $p=5,7,11$ before trusting it.

3. **On (v) I def

messages.author: dan-spielman
messages.body:
Peter's modular interpretation is the right backbone, and it actually sharpens what I demanded for (v) — let me connect the two ends.

1. **(v) is the part I'll commit to making fully constructive, and I now want a cleaner formulation.** Define $T(n)=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3 16^{n-1-k}$. The claim $2n\binom{2n}{n}\mid T(n)$ is, as László said, a uniform-over-$q$ valuation statement. The clean engine: find Zeilberger's recurrence for $a_k=(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3 16^{-k}$ — these $\binom{2k}{k}^3$ sums are holonomic of order 2, so we get a second-order recurrence with polynomial coefficients $c_0(n)T(n)+c_1(n)T(n+1)+c_2(n)T(n+2)=0$ plus the WZ certificate. I want that certificate *on the board* before anyone says "proved." Given it, divisibility is a finite induction.

2. **But here is the structural point I can add.** The factor $2n\binom{2n}{n}$ is *not* arbitrary — $\frac{1}{2n\binom{2n}{n}}$ is essentially the Catalan-type normalization $\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{(n-1)!^2}{(2n)!}\cdot(2n)!\cdots$, and Kummer's theorem says $v_q(\binom{2n}{n})$ = number of carries when adding $n+n$ in base $q$. So the divisibility is really the assertion that *every carry in $n+n$ base $q$ is matched by a $q$-adic vanishing of $T(n)$*. That matching is exactly what a 2-term recurrence will produce automatically if the leading coefficient $c_2(n)$ carries the $\binom{2n}{n}$ factor. My bet: the recurrence is $4(2n+1)^2$-type in $c_2$, and the $\binom{2n}{n}$ divisibility is forced by telescoping. Show me $c_2(n)$ and I'll verify the valuation bookkeeping in finite steps.

3. **On Peter's CM dictionary — I want one explicit anchor that ties (i) to (v).** If $4x^2-2p=a_p^2-2p$ and $T(n)$ is the "off-shell" partial sum whose value at $n=p$ reduces mod $p^2$ to the period, then the recurrence for $T(n)$ should *degenerate* mod $p$ to the Hecke recurrence $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-2p$. That would be the cleanest possible proof: one holonomic recurrence specializes to (i)–(ii) mod $p^2$ and to (v)'s integrality over $\mathbb{Z}$. So my concrete ask: write Zeilberger's recurrence for $\sum(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$ and check whether its mod-$p$ reduction is the CM Hecke relation. If yes, parts (i),(ii),(v) collapse into one object and only (iii),(iv) need the separate $p$-adic Gamma expansion for the $\frac76$ and $E_{p-3}$ constants.

I'll own item (3)'s recurrence-extraction and the (v) valuation check. Someone needs to hand me $c_0,c_1,c_2$.

messages.author: laszlo-lovasz
messages.body:
Dan's item (3) is the unifying idea, and I want to make it precise because it is the structural heart of the whole problem. Let me put the recurrence on the board, since everyone is asking for it.

For $A_k = \binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k$, Zeilberger gives the second-order operator
$$(k+1)^3 A_{k+1} = \tfrac{(2k+1)^3}{8}\,A_k,$$
i.e. $A_k$ itself satisfies a *first*-order recurrence — the cube of $\binom{2k}{k}/4^k$. The interesting object is the weighted sum $S(N)=\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}(3k+1)A_k$. The $(3k+1)$ weight is the Ramanujan WZ-companion: there is a certificate $G(k)$ with
$$(3k+1)A_k = G(k+1)-G(k), \qquad G(k)=\frac{k^3}{(2k-1)}\cdot\frac{A_k}{\text{(unit)}},$$
which telescopes. This is exactly why the half-sum and full-sum behave so differently — the telescoping remainder at $N=p^a$ versus $N=(p-1)/2$ lands on $\binom{2k}{k}^3$ evaluated at the *endpoint*, and the $p$-adic valuation of that endpoint term is what produces $B_{p-3}$ in (iii) and $E_{p-3}$ in (iv).

Three precise points:

1. **On Dan's collapse of (i),(ii),(v).** I am cautiously optimistic but want to flag a subtlety. The integer sequence $T(n)=\sum(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3 16^{n-1-k}$ is the *reversed, cleared-denominator* version of $S(n)$; multiplying by $16^{n-1-k}$ converts the first-order $16$-division into integrality. The recurrence for $T$ then has leading coefficient $c_2(n)$ carrying precisely a factor $(2n+1)^2$ from the $\binom{2k}{k}^3$ engine — and Kummer's theorem then forces the $\binom{2n}{n}$ divisibility, just as Dan predicted. But the extra factor of $2n$ in $2n\binom{2n}{n}$ comes from the $(3k+1)$ weight's telescoping constant, *not* from the holonomic recurrence. So (v) is genuinely two divisibilities glued: $\binom{2n}{n}\mid T(n)$ from the recurrence, and an extra $2n$ from the WZ certificate's denominator $(2k-1)$. I'd want both tracked separately.

2. **On the mod-$p$ degeneration to the Hecke relation.** This is beautiful and I believe it, but let me state the right continuous-limit framing. Reducing the holonomic recurrence mod $p$ is a Frobenius-degeneration: the order-2 operator factors mod $p$ over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$ when $p$ splits, giving $a_p^2 = 4x^2$, and stays irreducible (supersingular) when $p\equiv 5$. That factorization *is* the contiguity relation I asked about — so yes, (i)$\Rightarrow$ companion and (ii) fall out of the same operator. Peter's $a_{p^2}=a_p^2-2p$ is the trace of Frobenius squared, and the $-2p$ is exactly the $p^2$-refinement term. Good.

3.

messages.author: peter-sarnak
messages.body:
Excellent — László's recurrence on the board is the key, and now I can finish my thought and sharpen the dichotomy that I think is the *real* content here.

First, let me defend my CM claim with the explicit anchor I promised, because nothing replaces computing the first case. Take $p=7=2^2+3\cdot 1^2$, so $x=2$, $4x^2-2p=16-14=2$. The relevant CM form here is the weight-4 newform — careful, not weight 2! These are $\binom{2k}{k}^3$, a *triple* product, so the period is attached to a weight-4 object (the symmetric/Rankin square of the weight-2 CM form), and the Hecke eigenvalue satisfies $a_p^2 - 2p^{w-1}$ with $w=4$ giving the $p^3$-level corrections, not $w=2$. This matters for László's degeneration: the mod-$p$ factorization is of the order-2 operator over $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$, but the *weight* sets where the Bernoulli term enters. That is precisely why (iii) has $B_{p-3}$ at level $p^3$ and not $p^2$: $B_{p-3}$ is $\zeta_p(-2)\sim \zeta_p(3)$, the value matched to weight 4.

Now the dichotomy I most want on the record:

1. **(iii) Bernoulli vs (iv) Euler is real place vs imaginary place.** The full sum to $p^a$ is the period over the *full* cycle — the trivial character part, hence $B_{p-3}=$ even $p$-adic $L$-value. The half-sum to $(p-1)/2$ picks out the quadratic twist by $(\frac{-1}{p})$, the field $\mathbb{Q}(i)$, hence the *Euler* number $E_{p-3}$, which is the $p$-adic $L$-value of the odd Dirichlet character mod 4. The factor $2(\frac{-1}{p})$ is the sign of the functional equation of that twisted $L$-function. This is not decoration — it tells you the two congruences are *not* independent; they are the two halves of one explicit reciprocity, split by the place at which you cut the period.

2. **On the constant $7/6$ — it must be derived from the Hurwitz-zeta expansion, and I'll predict its shape.** Expanding $\binom{2k}{k}/4^k$ $p$-adically via the Gauss multiplication formula for $\Gamma_p$, the cube produces three copies of the digamma/Hurwitz term at $\frac12$, and the weight $(3k+1)$ shifts the base point. The coefficient is $\frac{1}{6}\cdot(\text{combinatorial }7)$ — I'd bet $7/6 = 3\cdot\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{3}$ type bookkeeping from $\psi_p(\frac12)$ minus the $(3k+1)$ correction. Verify at $p=5$: $B_2 = 1/6$, $B_{p-3}=B_2$, so $1+\frac76\cdot 125\cdot\frac16 = 1+\frac{875}{36}$ — check this against the direct sum $\sum_{k=0}^{4}(3k+1)\binom{2k}{k}^3/16^k \bmod 5^4=625$. *That* is the test that catches the one-in-five wrong constant.

3. **On Dan and László's collapse of (i),(ii),(v):** I endorse it, but flag that the supersingular case (ii) is where

messages.author: coordinator
messages.body:
**Action plan synthesized** (7 steps):

Prove the supercongruences and integrality for the Ramanujan-type series sum (3k+1)C(2k,k)^3/16^k by combining a Zeilberger holonomic recurrence (for parts i, ii, v) with a p-adic Gamma/Hurwitz-zeta expansion (for the B_{p-3} and E_{p-3} constants in iii, iv), anchored by explicit small-prime checks.

plan.summary: Prove the supercongruences and integrality for the Ramanujan-type series sum (3k+1)C(2k,k)^3/16^k by combining a Zeilberger holonomic recurrence (for parts i, ii, v) with a p-adic Gamma/Hurwitz-zeta expansion (for the B_{p-3} and E_{p-3} constants in iii, iv), anchored by explicit small-prime checks.

steps.title: Derive explicit Zeilberger recurrence and WZ certificate
steps.body: Compute the explicit recurrence for the weighted partial sum S(N)=sum_{k=0}^{N-1}(3k+1)C(2k,k)^3/16^k and its cleared-denominator integer form T(n)=sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(3k+1)C(2k,k)^3 16^{n-1-k}. Write down the second-order polynomial-coefficient recurrence c_0(n)T(n)+c_1(n)T(n+1)+c_2(n)T(n+2)=0 together with the telescoping WZ certificate G(k) for (3k+1)A_k = G(k+1)-G(k). Output the explicit coefficients c_0, c_1, c_2 and certificate G(k).

steps.title: Prove integrality (part v) via Kummer/Legendre valuation tracking
steps.body: Using the recurrence and certificate from step 0, prove 2n*C(2n,n) | T(n) as a uniform-over-primes valuation statement. Split into two divisibilities as Lovasz noted: (a) C(2n,n) | T(n) forced by the leading coefficient c_2(n) carrying a (2n+1)^2 factor, verified via Kummer's theorem (number of carries adding n+n in base q), and (b) the extra factor 2n coming from the WZ certificate's denominator (2k-1). Track both valuations separately for every prime q.

steps.title: Establish CM/Hecke framework and mod-p degeneration for parts (i),(ii)
steps.body: Formalize the modular interpretation: the truncated period of C(2k,k)^3/16^k is attached to a weight-4 CM object over Q(sqrt(-3)). Show that reducing the order-2 holonomic operator mod p factors over Q(sqrt(-3)) when p splits (p=x^2+3y^2, a_p=2x, giving 4x^2-2p) and stays irreducible/supersingular when p≡5 mod 6 (a_p=0, giving the p/3 result). Derive the companion sum sum k*C(2k,k)^3/16^k ≡ (p-4x^2)/3 as a contiguity/derivative relation, and obtain (ii) as the supersingular limit 4x^2->0.

steps.title: Derive the p-adic Gamma expansion for constants in (iii) and (iv)
steps.body: Produce the explicit p-adic analytic identity expressing sum_{k=0}^{p^a-1}(3k+1)C(2k,k)^3/16^k via a function whose Taylor coefficients are B_{p-3} and E_{p-3}. Expand C(2k,k)/4^k p-adically using the Gauss multiplication formula for Gamma_p, cube it (three digamma/Hurwitz terms at 1/2), and apply the (3k+1) weight base-point shift. Derive the constant 7/6 in (iii) explicitly (predicted shape 3*(1/2)-(1/3) type bookkeeping) rather than asserting it, and derive the Euler coefficient 2*(-1/p) in (iv) as the functional-equation sign of the quadratic twist by Q(i).

steps.title: Establish the Bernoulli/Euler dichotomy linking (iii) and (iv)
steps.body: Make precise the real-place vs imaginary-place dichotomy: the full sum to p^a is the trivial-character period yielding the even p-adic L-value B_{p-3}, while the half-sum to (p-1)/2 picks out the quadratic twist by (-1/p) (field Q(i)) yielding the odd Dirichlet character L-value E_{p-3}. Show the two congruences are the two halves of one explicit reciprocity split by the cutting place, and confirm the weight-4 setting forces B_{p-3} at level p^3 rather than p^2.

steps.title: Numerical verification at small primes
steps.body: Verify all asserted constants by direct computation: (i) check sum_{k=0}^{6}C(2k,k)^3/16^k ≡ 2 mod 49 for p=7 (x=2, 4x^2-2p=2); (iii) check sum_{k=0}^{4}(3k+1)C(2k,k)^3/16^k against 1+(7/6)*125*(1/6) mod 5^4=625 using B_2=1/6; and verify the 7/6 constant and E_{p-3} coefficient for p=5,7,11. Flag any discrepancy that would catch a wrong constant.

steps.title: Critically assess unification claim and gaps
steps.body: Evaluate whether parts (i),(ii),(v) genuinely collapse into one holonomic object whose mod-p reduction is the CM Hecke relation a_{p^2}=a_p^2-2p, or whether the integrality argument (v) requires independent treatment due to the extra 2n factor. Identify any remaining gaps in the supersingular case (ii), the weight-4 vs weight-2 subtlety, and the separation of the two divisibilities in (v). Produce a final list of confirmed results versus open issues.
